steps.title: State Gayah's exact claim
steps.content: Eliminating left turns at busy urban intersections will improve traffic flow and, as a result, reduce overall travel times — even if drivers sometimes have to travel a slightly longer distance. Support has to show that after such a restriction, drivers actually accomplish more in less time despite the longer routes.

steps.title: Test the candidate that matches the prediction
steps.content: Choice C reports that after a city eliminated left turns at busy intersections, delivery drivers reached more addresses per day on average and delivered more packages annually. More addresses per day with the same driving hours means travel time per stop dropped — flow improved enough to overcome any added distance. That is exactly the outcome Gayah predicts.

steps.title: Eliminate the others by why they miss
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'seven out of ten survey respondents agreed... easier to navigate': this describes adding left-turn signals, the opposite of Gayah's proposal — misread to treat this as support. ✗
  • Choice B — 'drivers wait longer to make a left turn at intersections without left-turn signals': compares two ways of allowing left turns; Gayah is talking about eliminating them entirely. Wrong intervention. ✗
  • Choice C — 'drivers... reach more addresses in the city daily... deliver more packages there annually': directly shows improved flow and reduced effective travel time after the elimination. ✓
  • Choice D — 'school buses... took on average two minutes longer to complete their routes after the restriction': longer route times after elimination directly contradict the claim. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
